reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Employer interest in talent pipeline quality and labour-market matching; digital-economy sector dependence on high-skill intake; school-to-work transition equity as public good (reduced mismatch waste; broader skill-base availability to employers).
reactions.interest: Employers and the digital/tech sector — particularly those dependent on scarce STEM talent and on school-to-work transition efficiency.
reactions.public_good_frame: A talent pipeline that surfaces high-ability pupils regardless of family background reduces employer search costs, improves match quality, and expands the addressable talent pool. Earlier identification of ability among low-SES pupils increases supply of skilled entrants available to the labour market.
reactions.argument: S1 conditionally serves employer interest IF the redesigned admission instrument reliably identifies ability independent of test-prep access, AND if regional quotas do not exclude high-ability applicants from affluent backgrounds (which would reduce absolute intake of top talent). The mechanism preserves selective tracks — which employers value as accelerated pipelines for advanced STEM and digital roles — while widening the talent-identification net. This is operationally compatible with employer demand. However, S1 depends on three unverified conditions: (1) a working SES indicator at application time; (2) a genuinely low-coachability redesigned exam (not merely a rebalanced one); (3) outreach that broadens the applicant pool. If any fails, composition changes but talent identification does not improve, and employers inherit the same filtering problem. The compliance-audit cost is modest relative to the talent-pipeline gain if the mechanism works.
reactions.condition_to_change: Lose support if: (a) the redesigned exam remains coachable and simply shifts preparation cost rather than eliminating it; (b) quotas are set so tightly that they reduce the absolute number of high-ability entrants (displacing affluent high-ability pupils without compensating gain in low-SES talent); (c) the SES indicator is not available or is gameable. Win support if: pilot data from the assessment redesign demonstrate genuinely reduced coaching sensitivity, AND longitudinal tracking shows low-SES high-ability pupils applying and succeeding at higher rates post-reform.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Employer interest in talent-identification timing and pipeline sustainability; digital-economy skills-formation needs; school-to-work equity and match quality.
reactions.interest: Employers and the digital/tech sector — particularly those dependent on early identification of STEM aptitude and on the sustainability of talent supply across cohorts.
reactions.public_good_frame: Delayed selection age combined with higher-quality general-school provision expands the window for ability identification and reduces misallocation of talent due to childhood SES noise. A common track to age 14 with advanced-pathway funding retains ambitious pupils in a resource-richer environment, improving talent identification quality. This serves both equity (fairer identification) and employer interests (better-matched skill supply).
reactions.argument: S2 is conditionally valuable to employers because it addresses a real talent-identification problem — early sorting at age 10/12 locks in achievement correlates of family background, not pure ability. Delayed selection to age 14 gives ability more time to emerge independent of early advantage. The advanced-programme funding in general schools is operationally compatible with employer demand IF it is genuine (not token) and IF it prevents middle-class exit that would shrink the peer-composition benefit. However, S2 depends critically on two unverified assumptions: (1) that advanced funding actually retains ambitious families in the common track rather than accelerating flight to non-state schools; (2) that displaced demand does not migrate to uncapped non-state selective places, offsetting the equity gain and leaving employers with a smaller state-system talent pool. The residual selective places under caps become mechanically more SES-skewed absent S1-type admission reform — a perverse effect from the employer perspective, since it concentrates talent-identification noise in the shrinking state-selective sector. S2 is therefore only coherent paired with S1-type admission reform of residual places. The recurring cost of advanced-programme funding is manageable; the political reversal risk (Polish precedent) is the larger concern for employers dependent on long-term pipeline stability.
reactions.condition_to_change: Lose support if: (a) longitudinal school-choice data show that advanced funding does not retain ambitious families in general schools and flight to non-state selective places rises; (b) the cap is reversed by a successor government, sunk transition costs are wasted, and employer confidence in policy stability erodes; (c) S1-type admission reform is not paired with S2, and residual selective places become more SES-skewed, reducing talent identification in the state system. Win support if: (a) pilot data from the phase-down in a comparable region show that advanced-programme funding retains composition diversity and does not trigger middle-class exit; (b) a cross-party pact or entrenchment mechanism demonstrably locks the trajectory against reversal; (c) S2 is legislatively paired with or followed by S1-type admission rules for residual places.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Employer interest in talent-pool size and teacher-quality constraints; digital-economy sector dependence on high-quality instruction; school-to-work transition efficiency across ability ranges.
reactions.interest: Employers and the digital/tech sector — particularly those dependent on breadth of talent supply, but also acutely dependent on teacher quality given current Hungarian shortages.
reactions.public_good_frame: Comprehensive schooling to age 14 with sustained teacher-quality and diagnostic-support investment maximises the likelihood of identifying and developing talent across the full ability range. This widens the addressable talent pool and reduces mismatch. However, the public-good case is conditional on whether the structural change is accompanied by actual teacher-quality gains or merely distributes a scarce teacher supply thinly across a larger, more heterogeneous system.
reactions.argument: S3 is the most strategically valuable scenario to employers IF it works — a comprehensive system with high-quality differentiated instruction would surface talent across the full SES range and allow later, better-informed tracking. The cross-national evidence (Finland, Poland) on delayed tracking is genuinely moderate. However, S3 is also the highest-risk scenario from an employer perspective, for three reasons: (1) It depends on teacher-quality investment that Hungary's current shortage makes highly uncertain — the 'bundling' with retraining assumes the state can simultaneously retrain a scattered teacher population while covering comprehensively heterogeneous classrooms. This is a load-bearing assumption; if the retraining is delayed or partial, comprehensive schools degrade into mixed-ability holding pens with lower instruction quality overall, harming both top and bottom of the distribution. (2) The asymmetric-peer-effect channel (the mechanism by which lower-SES pupils gain from proximity to high-achievers) is explicitly tagged 'contested' in the scenario inputs and Hungarian-specific evidence is weak. If this channel fails, the equity case collapses and S3 leaves employers with a broader but lower-quality talent pool. (3) The middle-class exit risk is highest under S3, and if sorting migrates to non-state schools en masse, the state system becomes a residual lower-SES track with degraded peer composition — the opposite of the intended effect. The 'fallback to S2' language in implementation steps signals that even the scenario designers are uncertain about political durability. For employers, this volatility is costly: sunk investment in new teacher training can be reversed, and the talent-identification system becomes unstable across government changes.
reactions.condition_to_change: Lose support if: (a) pilot data from the comprehensive model show that teacher quality degrades or remains stagnant despite retraining investment, or that retraining is delayed beyond the pilot phase; (b) longitudinal school-composition data show substantial middle-class migration to non-state schools, leaving the state comprehensive system with degraded peer composition; (c) a change of government reverses the structural reform, wasting retraining sunk costs and eroding employer confidence in policy stability. Win support if: (a) a pilot in 2-3 districts demonstrates that comprehensive instruction with differentiation training can match or exceed selective-track attainment for top-ability pupils in the comprehensive setting; (b) school-choice data show that middle-class families remain in well-resourced comprehensive schools; (c) a pre-legislative cross-party pact includes supermajority or statutory-lock provisions that credibly protect the reform against reversal; (d) teacher-supply studies show that the retraining pipeline can be sequenced without degrading ongoing instruction quality.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Employer interest in talent-identification quality and pipeline efficiency; digital-economy sector dependence on high-STEM entrants; school-to-work transition equity.
reactions.interest: Employers and the digital/tech sector — particularly those dependent on STEM talent and on talent-identification accuracy independent of family background.
reactions.public_good_frame: Structural sorting at age 10/12 is a talent-identification mechanism; compensation cannot offset the noise in that mechanism because the sorting itself was never about ability in the first place. Compensation masks the sorting effect without eliminating it. From an employer perspective, this trades temporary fiscal availability for permanent talent-identification degradation.
reactions.argument: S4 is fundamentally misaligned with employer interest because it leaves intact the early-sorting mechanism that produces the talent-identification problem employers face. The scenario's own uncertainties are explicit: 'no country has demonstrated [that compensation can offset rather than merely mask early-sorting effects] at Hungary's selection age.' This is not a minor empirical doubt — it is the single load-bearing assumption on which the entire public-good case rests. Without it, S4 is merely expensive compensation for a problem it does not solve. From an employer perspective: (1) Early sorting at age 10/12 embeds SES noise into the talent-identification system. Compensation can improve outcomes in disadvantaged schools, but it cannot untangle ability from background in the children already sorted into general tracks — the selection has already happened. The talent lost to misclassification in the early years cannot be recovered by later spending. (2) The recurring cost of TEIP-style targeting is explicitly flagged as 'budget-vulnerable' — it is the first line cut in fiscal consolidation. This means employers cannot rely on stable talent-supply patterns; the programme is politically fragile across cycles. (3) Sustained compensation risks legitimising early selection permanently, embedding sorting into the system as a structural feature that 'compensation' is then expected to remedy forever. This is expensive and makes the talent-identification system dependent on repeated fiscal decisions rather than structural design. (4) Teacher shortage, documented as strong, undermines both the standards rollout and the premia-based staffing of disadvantaged schools — the mechanism itself is operationally fragile. For employers dependent on STEM talent, S4 is a dead end: it spends heavily without improving the fundamental talent-identification problem, and it makes employers dependent on perpetual fiscal transfers that are politically exposed.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would require: (a) empirical evidence from Hungary or a directly comparable context that TEIP-style compensation at Hungary's selection age actually offsets (not merely masks) early-sorting effects, measured via longitudinal ability-matching or labour-market outcome tracking; (b) credible multi-party fiscal guarantee that compensation funding is protected from cyclical cuts across at least 15-year horizons (the timeline over which a cohort effect would be visible to employers); (c) evidence that teacher-shortage constraints do not prevent implementing standards and premia-based staffing simultaneously. As written, S4 does not meet any of these conditions. The scenario is internally contradictory on this point: it claims compensation as the mechanism while its own uncertainties state the mechanism is unproven.
